preFix.title: Cutting a scarf joint
preFix.subtitle: Join two lengths of timber end-to-end in a clean, strong splice
preFix.excerpt: How to cut a plain or lapped scarf joint to splice two lengths of timber: marking the angle, sawing and planing clean, gluing and clamping, and applications in skirting boards, architrave, fence rails, and structural repair.
preFix.sourceNotes: Newey and Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: A
scarf joint

allows two short lengths of timber to read as a single length. In joinery work (skirting boards, architrave, picture rails), the diagonal plane of the scarf is nearly invisible when painted. In structural repair, a
lapped scarf joint
splices a partially rotted rail or joist without removing the entire length.
When to use it
Joining two lengths of skirting or architrave where a single full-length piece would be too long to handle.
Splicing a partially rotted fence rail or arris rail where the post is sound.
Extending a stair handrail where a standard-length rail is too short.
Step-by-step: plain diagonal scarf
Step 1, mark the angle.

Mark a diagonal line across the timber using a sliding bevel set to approximately 45 degrees (or a shallower angle of 1:5 or 1:8 for a longer, stronger glue face). Use a marking knife for a precise scored line rather than pencil, which is too wide for accurate sawing.
Step 2, saw.
Saw along the marked line with a sharp tenon saw, keeping the blade just on the waste side of the knife line. The cut must be in a single plane; a twisted cut means the two pieces will not mate flat.
Step 3, plane and fit.

Plane the face of both halves with a sharp block plane to remove saw marks and ensure the two faces mate without gaps. Test-fit: the joint should close gap-free along its full length when held together by hand.
Step 4, glue and clamp.

Apply PVA to both faces, bring together, and clamp until set. Drive a single countersunk screw through the face of the joint (from the hidden side on a skirting board) to reinforce the glue bond and draw the two pieces tight.
Positioning the joint

In a skirting board or architrave, position the joint over a stud or nogging so both pieces can be nailed into the structure. A joint floating in open span relies on glue alone and tends to open with seasonal movement.
Common mistakes
Marking with pencil rather than a knife.
A pencil line has a width of about 0.5 mm; the saw blade and the pencil line together create a gap. Score the line with a marking knife.
Twisting the saw during the cut.
The faces of the scarf must be flat and in the same plane. Clamp a guide batten along the diagonal line to keep the saw aligned.

TECHNIQUE tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
